description: Apollo 10 Flight Journal - Day 5, part 21: Snoopy chases Charlie Brown

tokens: NASA played conservative when selecting the rendezvous method for the early two vehicle missions.
When choosing the best method of rendezvous, the final closure rate between the two spacecraft, and the lighting conditions at rendezvous were primary concerns.
They chose the coelliptic rendezvous method, with the LM in the primary, active role, and the CSM calculating and preparing to back up the manoeuvres should the LM run into trouble.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: To arrive at these final approach conditions, the rendezvous sequence was broken into three parts, CSI-Coelliptic Sequence Initiation, CDH-Constant Delta Height, TPI-Terminal Phase Initiation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: At the time of the first LM apolune following the insertion burn, the CSI manoeuvre effectively circularized the LM orbit.
Half an orbit after CSI, the CDH manoeuvre placed the LM in an orbit that was constantly 15 miles (27.8 km) below that of the CSM.
This gave the LM time to track the CSM using the rendezvous radar and calculate the parameters for the TPI manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The experience in rendezvous gained during the Gemini, two man, Earth-orbital missions, showed that the terminal phase of the rendezvous should commence approximately 130° behind the optimum location for the final rendezvous and docking.
The station keeping and docking needs to take place when the lighting is most appropriate.
Also they wanted to have the LM approach from a lower orbit, visible against the lunar surface as seen from the CSM, and the CSM appearing fixed against the celestial background as seen from the LM.
Most of the terminal phase took place in darkness, and the final braking phase in daylight, just after the orbital sunrise.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Following the TPI manoeuvre, the LM starts to climb up to the CSM orbital altitude.
There are two opportunities to make mid-course corrections if required, before arriving at the final braking phase.
Three braking manoeuvres were planned, carried out by the LM.
The LM at this stage was very light and extremely responsive to firings of its RCS.
The CSM could backup these manoeuvres if required, but was still considerably heavier and less responsive.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: During all of these manoeuvres, the crew of both spacecraft monitored to see if there was any need to adjust the orbital plane of the LM, for it to coincide with that of the CSM.
Any out-of-plane element could be adjusted for as part of the CSI, CDH or TPI manoeuvres, or a small separate manoeuvre between CSI and CDH.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: APS / RCS interconnect coming open and the RCS SOV vales being closed for the CSI burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The CSI burn using the LM RCS, draws its propellant from the APS tanks via the interconnect valves.
Cernan is now placing the System A & B ASC Feed 1 & 2 switches (4) in the Closed position.
These switches are all on panel 1.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: He will then put the System A & B Main SOV switches on panel 1, to the Open position, returning the RCS propellant feed from its own tanks.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Following the CSI maneuver, the AGS calculates that the LM is in a 48.2 x 41.6 nm [89.3 x 77.0 km] orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Both vehicles are showing the same range via the rendezvous radar, and the VHF ranging system.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Page 18of the LM Timeline Book shows details of the procedures the crew followed between CSI and CDH.
The steps to be taken for the cancelled plane change are included.
It includes the notations made by the crew at the time.
AGS related entries begin with the * symbol.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew continue to get an erroneous first readout when they call up P20, which they have to reject.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Flight Plan calls for Young to continue to track the LM using the CSM sextant, taking 3 marks over the next 2 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: TV coverage of the rendezvous and docking was left to crew discretion.
Stafford wants Young to be able to concentrate on the docking without being distracted by the TV.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The CSM docking target, also known as the LM-active docking target is used when the LM has to adopt the active vehicle role in the docking manoeuvre.
This was the primary method on Apollo 9, but on Apollo 10 and all subsequent Apollo missions the docking in lunar orbit was planned with the CSM in the active role.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Should a problem be encountered with the CSM, for example with the RCS, the LM can take over the active role for the final docking.

Following the rendezvous and station keeping, The commander, viewing through the LM overhead window, using the COAS, would align the two spacecraft using the CSM docking target which the CMP has mounted in the CSM window 4, the right hand rendezvous window.
The commander would perform the final closure between the two spacecraft, using the LM controls to complete the docking.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is referring back to the switching errors at staging.
See hiscomments from the postflight debrief.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is making the observation that the Z-axis tracking of the CSM by the LM, to point the rendezvous radar at the CSM, is using quite a lot of RCS propellant.
However the overall use of RCS propellant by the LM, proved to be 10% less than the pre-flight estimate.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is querying whether Cernan has the correct settings on the 16-mm sequence camera for interior filming.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This video was compiled by journal contributor Stephen Slater using the 16-mm sequence camera footage taken by Stafford and Cernan of each other within Snoopy's cabin and the DSEA onboard audio.
It includes footage of Stafford using the DSKY.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A low resolution versionis also available.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: CSM solo operations detailed Flight Plan
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Maintaining the LM attitude with the plus-X axis pointing directly towards the CSM for the rendezvous radar tracking is using quite a lot of RCS propellant.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Provision was made in the Flight Plan for a plane change manoeuvre, should it be required, between the CSI and CDH manoeuvres at a time nominated by the crew.
Cernan has suggested 104:15:00, if they feel they need it.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young is calculating the required plane change solution independently with the CMC using routine 36.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The 1st calculation of the required Delta-V at CDH is: plus-X, 0.3; plus-Y, 0.0; plus-Z, 1.9 fps (plus-X, 0.1; plus-Y, 0.0; plus-Z, 0.6 m/s) relative to the LVLH framework, Plus-X is the direction of flight, the orbital plane, Plus-Y is perpendicular to the orbital plane, to the right, Plus-Z is vertically down towards the Moon's center.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew are erroneously applying a sign change to the CSM out of plane value, thus believing that the out-of-plane values obtained by the two spacecraft are in opposite directions, and they believe to some degree cancel each other out, so they have decided to delay the out-of-plane correction at this time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: From theApollo 10 Mission Report:"A crew misunderstanding of the sign notation for this parameter existed and apparently resulted from the fact that all Command-Module mirror-image solutions for rendezvous require a sign reversal when used by the Lunar Module crew.

Since the determination of Lunar Module out-of-plane velocity is a separate routine in the Command Module Computer and not a mirror-image solution, this parameter should not be reversed in sign when used for comparison.
This fact had not been made clear enough before flight, and the crew was acting on what they believed to be the correct comparison procedure.
No difficulties were encountered by this misunderstanding and subsequent delay in the out-of-plane correction, since errors of this type do not increase (propagate)."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Actually, the agreement in sign of the out-of-plane velocity solutions was valid, since each vehicle computed precisely the same parameter, the out-of-plane velocity of the Lunar Module.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is recording the range to compare with backup manual charts in order to fine tune them for subsequent missions.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The W-matrix is used by the PGNS to allow for slight imperfections in the loaded state vector.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The only manoeuvre made using the APS on Apollo 9 whilst it was manned, was the CDH burn.
Apollo 9 did burn the APS again during the ascent engine burn to depletion for 362.3 seconds, once the LM ascent stage was finally separated from the CSM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young observed the DOI, DPS burn, using the CSM sextant.
He could see an orange plume.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young uses program P33 to compute a solution for the CSM to backup the CDH burn should the LM fail to perform this manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 104 hours, 18 minutes.
We're not quite 2 minutes away from acquisition of the Command Module.
We'll try to give you a - an explanation of what happened during the staging sequence.

The backup guidance system, the AGS or Abort Guidance System mode control switch was in Auto because the - the radar had been used to track the Command Module, and they were using the Auto mode for Reaction Control System attitude control.
Now at - the - this mode control switch should have been placed in Attitude Hold for staging, but it was not because that step had been omitted from the checklist.

So the control system maneuvered the Lunar Module to lock on to the Command Module as the computer directed, but that was not the proper attitude for staging, and the crew were not sure whether they went to pulse mode or to manual, straight manual control, but they went to one of those two modes to get out of that situation, and the staging went well.
We're about 30 seconds away from acquisition now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Young is continuing to take optical marks of the LM through the CSM sextant.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The altitude at staging was 33.4 nautical miles (61.9 km).
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Gene Cernan was the only crewman in the LM being monitored by the flight surgeon, and his heart rate during that staging sequence ranged from 105 to 129.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The current PGNS range rate, is 126 fps (38.4 m/s), which compares very closely with that for the AGS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You guys got 21 minutes and counting, about?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Young is confirming that the SM has been frugal in its use of RCS propellant during the spacecraft solo operations.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Prior to the LM insertion manoeuvre, CSM attitude control was performed primarily either with the DAP in wide deadband at rates of 0.2 or 0.5 deg/sec or with the pulse mode of the Stabilization and Control System (SCS).

With the wide deadband mode, additional pilot attention to spacecraft roll was required to maintain the preflight angles for nominal High Gain Antenna acquisition and lock-on and still provide the proper line-of-sight coverage for the rendezvous radar transponder.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Boy, this tracking the LM against the background of the Moon or something down there is really fantastic.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Everything went good, and we had a beautiful Earthrise as you came up from behind the horizon.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Need you Noun 81 numbers and your residuals.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: For CSI the Noun 81 numbers were 45, 43, 00.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Noun 81 displays the Delta-V applied to the LM by the CSI manoeuvre relative to the LVLH framework: plus-X, 45.0; minus-Y, 0.43; plus-Z, 0.0; all in fps (plus-X, 13.7; minus-Y, 0.13; plus-Z, 0.0 m/s).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now we're going to try and put you High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Did Snoop do a plane change?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Verb 06 Noun 75 displays the Delta-altitude, the time between CDH and TPI, and the difference in time between the nominal TPI and the latest TPI ignition time update.
See the entry at CDH -18:00 onpage 18of the CSI to CDH Timeline Book.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We thought we could do it then.
It's at the plane-change time; and at the plane-change time, which was 104:15, I had minus 1.1 and they had plus.
They had one in the opposite direction, so we decided not to do it there.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: This is Snoopy on High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We got you on High Gain, Snoop.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Wish we could say the same about the Omnis.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yes, all I did was look at you coming over the horizon, and I pointed High Gain up and down my Z-axis like I hoped I could; and man, there you were, and she locked on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We got you, and we got you counting down, and we see you 16 45.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Seems like every time we initialize or call a P20 for the first time, the first Mark we have to reject because it's got horrendous 5-digit numbers in it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: When in P20, the crew call up Verb 06 Noun 49 to display on the DSKY the Delta-R - range, XXXX.X nm, and Delta-V - rate of closure, XXXX.X fps.
The initial P20 LGC readout of range rate from the RR, is displayed as a set of random and very large values.See explanation at 101:08:47.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It looks like TPI - My last recycle [Noun 75], TPI moved away from us 2 minutes and 40 seconds.
I'm going to go and try calculating out plane-change here and compare it with yours, but it looks like we're in good shape.
I got Delta-H of 14.9 [nautical miles, 27.6 km].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That Delta-H is the differential in altitude between the Command Module and the Lunar Module, and Gene Cernan reads it as 14.9 nautical miles [27.6 km].
We'd like to be right around 15 nautical miles (27.8 km), so that's very good.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: In this next maneuver, the CDH...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're locked on with you all the way.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is confirming they have good rendezvous radar lock-on on the CSMCharlie Brown.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You're right in plane with us, Charlie Brown.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: One thing that looks real good to us, is the Rendezvous Radar temperature has kept fairly cool.
In fact, where it is now, at 80, is about as hot as it's gotten all day.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Page 19of the LM Timeline Book shows details of the procedures the crew followed between CSI and CDH.
It includes the notations made by the crew at the time.
AGS related entries begin with the * symbol.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Our plane change is NG, so we're not going to burn ours.
If it sounds good, we'll look at it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Young has computed his own value for the LM out-of-plane; minus-Y, 4.2 fps (1.28 m/s).
At this point the crew have little confidence in these values, so they decide to delay any out-of-plane correction until TPI.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: John, you don't want to burn it, do you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're going to ignore it here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You can take it out quite easily.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Ours was in the opposite direction even with the sign changed, but let's not burn it.
You're right down to belly band, babe.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The crew are still under the misapprehension, that the out-of-plane values for the CSM have the sign reversed.
Young is pointing out that any out-of-plane errors can still be taken out during later manoeuvres.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is recording the minus 10-minute range for the backup manual chart comparison.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we're 10 minutes away from the CDH maneuver burn that will make the differential altitude constant.
Be a very small maneuver on the order of 2 to 2½ feet per second (0.6 to 0.76 m/sec).
The ground solution is 2.3 feet per second (0.7 m/sec).
The crew is now getting solution from their computer.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Cernan has obtained these CDH values from the onboard backup charts.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: My [Noun] 81 and a minus 0.5, 0, and minus 2.9 for P33.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Young is computing the LM CDH values using the P33 in the AGC in the CSM.
His computed values are; minus-X, 0.5; plus-Y, 0.0; minus-Z, 2.9; all in fps (minus-X, 0.15; plus-Y, 0.0; minus-Z, 0.88 m/s), all relative to the LVLH framework.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is comparing the PGNS CDH values with those derived from the onboard backup charts, and they are in close agreement.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is calling up Verb 47 to initiate routine 47-AGS initialization.
This will copy the current LM state vector form the LGC to the AGS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is calling for P41, to compute the IMU orientation and preferred vehicle attitude for the upcoming CDH RCS manoeuvre, then calling Verb 06 Noun 86, the LGC displays the velocity to be gained in the three axis (in the Local Vertical framework), X, Y, Z during the CDH burn.
The values displayed are plus-X, 0.1; plus-Y, 0.0; plus-Z, 3.0; all in fps (plus-X, 0.03; plus-Y, 0.0; plus-Z, 0.9 m/s).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is the nicest CDH - This is the nicest CDH solution we ever got.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Using P33, Young has computed the CSM CDH solution for the final time, if required.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Looks like our CSI solution and the total targeting was just fantastic.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The Control officer reports that all of the LM consumables are in good shape.
We're showing a range now of 87 miles (161 km) between the LM and the CSM.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Going to do this in AGS.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We'll give the old AGS a try again.
[Laughter.] We'll make sure we're at Attitude Hold.
We knew what was wrong before.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The crew want to ensure they don't make the same mistake with the attitude control switch settings, that they made at LM staging.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'm not reading him at all.
Notice - We noticed he bypassed his roll maneuver at about 104:33.
We recommend he manually roll 180 before he does his P20 Auto maneuver over, out to the burn.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The CSM Flight Plan calls forCharlie Brownto roll 180° just before AOS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: John, we're at 3:43, 3:42, counting down to the burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Young is now preparing the CSM in case he has to make the CDH manoeuvre He calls up P41, RCS thrusting program and aligns the GDC.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is using program P34 to update the TPI ignition time.
He is letting Young know that the time the LGC is giving is approximately 2 minutes later than the ignition time voiced to them as part of the CSI PAD update at 103:14:21.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're in AGS, Min deadband attitude hold.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The actual residual errors following the CDH burn relative to the LVLH framework were plus-X, 0.1; plus-Y, 0.0; minus-Z, 0.1; all in fps (plus-X, 0.03; plus-Y, 0.0; minus-Z, 0.03 m/s).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And did you copy the residuals?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, go back up, John, and we'll be all set to track.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford wants Young to maneuver the CSM back to radar tracking attitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we have a solid lock-on, John.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I've had you in reflected sunlight out there - for about - for about 90 miles (167 km) on.
It's just about the same as around the Earth when we used to see the Agena, but I've got you as a little yellow dot out there, and Ive been tracking you since about 90 miles (167 km).
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And Ive got only one eye power.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Hey, John, after aligning this platform with the AOT, I can see why your eyes are - why you're saying what you're saying.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're right over by Moltke and the landing site again.
Getting a view of it now from 45 miles and again - just extrapolated from below.
It looks like we got about 25 to 30 percent clear area in there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're coming up to the craters Sabine and Ritter, and we can see U.S.
1 here, and the sides of U.S.
1 again are rounded and [garble] but the whole thing is dropped down just like reported before.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Houston, do you have a good gouge on...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Do you have a good gouge on the setting for internal film to use outside?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan believes he may only have internal colour film for the 16-mm sequence camera, and needs to know the f-stop offset for using it to shoot outside views.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Use two f-stops higher, Snoop, on that film; and, Tom, if you've got a chance to talk a minute, could you describe Landing Site 2 from 8 miles (14.8 km)?
We didn't have you in communications at that time.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Say you want me to describe the Landing Site 2 again?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We can get it later, Snoop.
We were out of communications with you at that time, but we will get it later.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The approach end looks lots smoother than some of the Orbiter photos show.
It's still estimate 25 to 30 per cent, say, semi clear area for - if the LM has enough hover time, at least from what we can see at 50,000 feet, it should not be a problem.
However, if you come down in the wrong area and you don't have the hover time you are going to have to shove off.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We got solid lock-on now, and we're working on P34.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're showing constant Delta altitude of 14.94 miles (27.67 km) between the two vehicles.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Would you relay that, if he's done his 180 roll, to try the High Gain for us.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is strictly as a relay.
If youve done completed your 180 degree roll, will you try your High Gain for Houston?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: If you use any different f-stops on the film, we'd like for you to mark it so we can process it right.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That ought to be a ball.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll try, Charlie; I'll do my best.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I was just - had- Don't worry about it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, you guys are floating on the world out there sideways.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: As the Earth came up, on this Earth day, I guess you would call it, the north pole was to the right and the south pole was to the left, and looks like - see a lot of clouds over the Pacific Ocean.
Didn't take much time to notice, but it was a beautiful sight.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Charlie, I don't know how the Big Man must see things, but if his view is any better than ours, it's got to be fantastic.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'd like to get a sunset time.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It happens so fast around here, I ought to be instantaneously aware of it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We got a time for you for sunset: 104 and 58.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I show you at 64.89 miles (120.18 km).
How do you agree with that, Snoop?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We show you about 64.6 (miles) (119.6 km) now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: For the record, we have been operating with Charlie Brown the whole time while he's been on VHF ranging, in ICS/PTT, and our hot mike apparently does not bother John at all; and we've had such good luck S-band with you folks down there that we've had hot mike a very short period of time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The ICS/PTT (Intercommunication subsystem/Push to talk) communication setting only allows voice transmission on the voice actuated circuit (VOX) when the PTT switch on either the astronauts umbilical carrier or ACA hand controller is depressed.
This mode was selected on the audio Mode switch on panel 8 for the CDR or panel 12 for the LMP, was placed in the ICS/PTT position.
The term 'hot mike' refers to when the crew are transmitting voice in VOX.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan's statement that the use of a 'hot mike' in the LM does not bother the CMP, is not quite borne out by the mission report.
The VHF ranging system was being operated by the CMP.
The mission report states 'All acquisitions were performed with a "hot-mike" configuration in the Lunar Module, which resulted in two false acquisitions.
Both false indications were readily noted by the Command Module Pilot and reacquisition was accomplished normally'.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That S-band antenna makes noise, but it sure does track.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're at 60 nautical miles (111 km) closing and R dot looks real fine.
I'm sure you're reading it down there.
Everything looks real good from here, and I still don't have his flashing light from this distance of 60 miles (111 km).
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We got you plotting right down the line on your charts.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll turn on these running lights and EVA lights too, Tom.
Maybe you can - can see them when you get closer.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: During the final rendezvous and docking, the LM crew need to be able to make a gross determination of the CSM attitude.
This is aided by the CSM running lights.
The running lights consist of 8 lights on the service module, two green, two red, and four amber.
Four of these lights are located on the forward bulkhead, approximately half way between each of the axes.
The remaining four lights are located 6 inches (150 mm) forward of the service module rear bulkhead, also half way between the axes.
The two lights in the upper right quadrant are green, the two lights in the upper left quadrant are red, and the four lights in the lower quadrants are all amber.
The LM crew can determine the CSM attitude depending on what combination of lights they can see.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We show that as 120 feet per second (36.6 m/sec) and closing.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: CSM solo operations detailed Flight Plan
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Snoopy, you got a later TPI time?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: AGS comes out with a TPI at an angle of 26.51, of 24.4 to initiate, 55.9 to total, and that time is just about the same time I'm looking at right now of 105:23:20.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan has been using the AGS to calculate a TPI solution.
To call up the TPI Execute Routine, he called up address 410 on the DEDA, and made an entry of +40000.
Then he called up address 310 and enters the TPI ignition time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The AGS can now display the values it has calculated.
Calling address 303 will display the line of sight (LOS) angle between the LM local horizontal at TPI ignition and the CSM, 26.6° being the optimum angle.

Address 267 displays the total Delta-V required from the TPI manoeuvre to initiate the final climb to rendezvous, and address total Delta-V to be gained from the TPI burn and the following midcourse corrections.
Finally address 373 displays the AGS calculated TPI ignition time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: John, our new TPI time is 105:23:06.01; 105:23:06.01.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Using program P34, the LGC calculates the TPI ignition time for the desired LOS elevation angle.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's only moved up towards us about 12 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Coming up on 52, say, 0.3 miles [96.9 km] or so.
I still don't have you in the sight.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Did you get your final TPI time?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're not going to push final Comp until 12 minutes, but the latest one we got is what I just gave you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The final computation of the precise TPI ignition time is made at ignition minus 12 minutes, using P34.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, that thing is still calculating.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We've suddenly seen on this last recycle through 6 feet per second [1.8 m/sec] out of plane.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Do you have somebody that can read our erasable - check what our radar angle bias is?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: My TPI time - my TPI time - this later, is 105:23:16.79.
How's that for a good correlation.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Young is independently calculating the LM TPI ignition time, using the CMC program P74.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Your radar bias is in the noise.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If you get a chance, we'd like to look at...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'd like you to bring your Logic up so you can dock anytime.
And so we can give you a Go for the arm and also, I did you get a chance to cycle through the tunnel vent valve to LM Press?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: MCC-H are checking whether Young has had a chance to cycle the docking tunnel vent valve, in the vain hope that whatever is blocking it will be ejected.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The - you want the - SECS breakers are coming On, and the Pyro A and B battery breakers are going In.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You are Go for Pyro Arm at your convenience.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The SECS (Sequence Events Control System) circuit breakers on panel 8 provides DC power from battery bus A to the logic A bus (and battery bus B to logic bus B) in the master event sequence controller when the Seq Events Control System switches 1 & 2 are placed in the Logic position.
The Arm, A & B circuit breakers, also on panel 8, apply DC power from battery bus A & B to the Seq Events Cont Sys-Pyro Arm switches A and B.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Once the circuit breakers are pushed in, MCC-H can confirm the connections to the buses are satisfactory and give the Go for Pyro Arm.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The CMP, nearer to the time of the final rendezvous and docking, will place the Seq Events Cont System, Logic switches in the 1 & 2 position, and the Pyro Arm switches in the A and B position.
This action energizes the motor switches in the Lunar Docking Events Controller, which controls the docking probe retraction.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When you get your solution, we're interested in the out-of-plane part, so call it down to us, would you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: My Noun 81 is minus 21.8, plus 4½, and plus 10.14,
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Young is using the CSM AGC P34 to make his own calculations of the required Delta-V values, that the LM should burn at the TPI manoeuvre.
The resulting values are; minus-X, 21.8; plus-Y, 4.5; plus-Z, 10.14; all in fps (minus-X, 6.6; plus-Y, 1.4; plus-Z, 3.1 m/s).
The LM will adopt the out-of-plane plus-Y value.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, our new TPI time is 105:22:56.19.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Why don't you give me an LOS time and an AOS while you've got it, while we got a chance.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And Charlie Brown, Your LOS about the same.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Your LOS about the same, Charlie Brown, and AOS also.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll get them [garble] for you later.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm finally starting to see your flashing light, very faintly at 42 miles (77.8 km).
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Young now prepares for an SPS backup burn, using P40, should the LM not be able to perform the TPI manoeuvre.
He then manoeuvres to the desired attitude if the SPS burn is required.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Here's my Noun 81 for you that we're going to burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Noun 81 values from the PGNS for the TPI manoeuvre are; plus-X, 21.7; minus-Y, 4.5; minus-Z, 9.6; all in fps (plus-X, 6.6; minus-Y, 1.4; minus-Z, 2.9 m/s).
This incorporates the minus-Y 4.5 fps (1.4 m/s) out-of-plane correction.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're pitching down to put our X-axis towards you for the Auto maneuver from TPI.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're at burn attitude, Charlie Brown.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: This will be another Reaction Control System burn but the propellant will be fed from the Ascent Propulsion System tanks through the interconnect.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And Charlie Brown, our charts agree very closely, so we're Go.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: My numbers agree with your numbers.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Then, I guess were all in agreement, then.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: TPI burn is made using the LM RCS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And Snoopy's pitching back up to acquire.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The LM is pointing the plus-Z axis towards the CSM to acquire it using the Rendezvous Radar.
The LM crew are now focusing on Rendezvous Radar tracking to establish what trajectory errors exist following the TPI burn.

P35 is used by the LM to compute the required Delta-V and other initial conditions required by the LGC for LM execution of the next midcourse correction of the transfer phase of an active LM rendezvous.
Two opportunities are available for midcourse corrections if required, 15 and 30 minutes after TPI.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You can't believe how noisy those thrusters are.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It sounds like being inside a big rain tub with about 2-inch (5-cm) hail beating all over you.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Pitching up to give you radar target here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Young pitches the CSM up to allow P20 rendezvous radar tracking and tracking of the LM optically through the sextant.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: AndSnoopy's now on an intercept trajectory toCharlie Brown.
And he burned his own solution which agreed very closely within a few tenths with the ground solution.
He burned Delta-V of 24.1 feet per second (7.3 m/sec) at 105 hours, 22 minutes, 56 seconds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Young is running P35, CSM transfer phase midcourse targeting program.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We have solid lock, and first update appears real good.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Range rate 125 feet per second (38 m/s).
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I'm taking you off of High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That one felt like it was coming inside.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I can't see any flashing at all.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And this is Apollo Control at 105 hours, 32 minutes.
BothCharlie BrownandSnoopyhave gone behind the Moon, and we have Loss Of Signal.
When we next see them, they should be station keeping; getting ready to dock.

We're showing the Command Module, Command Service ModuleCharlie Brown, in a 63.37-nautical-mile (117.36 km) orbit, andSnoopyis on an intercept trajectory.Snoopyperformed two good burns during the time we had acquisition, the Constant Delta Height maneuver at which time we had a Constant Delta Height following that maneuver 14.94 nautical miles (27.67 km), and then the terminal phase itself completed just a short time ago.
The - there are two midcourse corrections programmed if needed.

The nominal Delta-V is zero in both of those, and Charlie Duke just remarked that just beforeSnoopywent around the corner, he heard Tom Stafford say they were just taking a mark, and they were right on.
So they may or may not do either of those midcourses.
And then the terminal phase is finalization.
The braking maneuvers will be performed behind the Moon, and at acquisition time, they should be station keeping within a few feet of each other.
Acquisition for theCharlie Brown; 106 hours, 18 minutes, 39 seconds; forSnoopy; 106 hours, 18 minutes, 55 seconds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The LM DSEA voice and data recorder, stopped recording at 104:44:40.
Therefore no transcript exists of the crew activity in both spacecraft during the final approach, mid-course corrections, braking and station keeping.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The first midcourse correction was carried out at 105:37:56, using the RCS.
Delta-V values; plus-X, 0.0; minus-Y, 0.4; plus-Z, 1.2; total 1.27 (all fps) (plus-X, 0.0; minus-Y, 0.12; plus-Z, 0.36; total 0.39 m/s).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crews commented on the midcourse corrections in the post mission crew debrief.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan, from the1969 Technical debrief:"P35 and P20 worked like a gem.
Our updates, which we looked at a number of times, were zero in range and zero in velocity.
Our midcourse corrections came out very small.
The first time they were minus 0.8, minus 0.4, and plus 0.8 (fps) (minus-X, 0.24; minus-Y, 0.12; plus-Z, 0.24 m/s).
We were plotting right up the pike; range rate was nominal."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford, from the1969 Technical debrief:"We were debating whether to make the first midcourse or not, but we decided to go ahead and do it since we wanted to evaluate the system from a performance standpoint."
transcript.speaker: Commentary
